coredata.dc:title: Structural design of carbon dots/porous materials composites and their applications
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jiancang
Xiao, Guangchun
Duan, Gaigai
Wu, Yongzhong
Zhao, Xiujian
Gong, Xiao
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), an emerging fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, have attracted great attention in recent year due to their features such as unique optical property, good biocompatibility and low cost and easy synthesis. When CDs are integrated with porous materials, it not only compensates for the shortcomings of monocomponent CDs in applications, but also produces new functionalities through synergistic interactions to extend new applications. At present, CDs have been assembled with various porous materials such as zeolites, carbonaceous porous materials, porous graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), mesoporous SiO2, porous metallic compounds and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), which exhibit excellent performance and a wide range of applications. Therefore, recent research advances in CDs/porous materials composites should be presented to facilitate further development of the field. In this article, the preparation methods, porous structures, and properties of CDs/porous materials composites are classified and presented. Then, the applications of CDs/porous materials composites in optics, biomedicine, sensing, electrochemistry, and photocatalysis are also discussed. Finally, current challenges and future directions are briefly discussed to facilitate the next development of CDs/porous materials composites.

Porous materials
Nanocomposites

row.entry: Arc discharge: arc discharge, sonication (5 min), ultracentrifugation (4 h, 120000 g), ultrafilter
Laser ablation: Q-switched Nd: YAG laser (1064 nm, 10 Hz), argon, acid treatment, passivation by PEG1500N
Electrochemical carbonization: graphite rod, ultrapure water, static potentials (15–60 V), 120 h
Chemical oxidation: sulfuric acid:nitric acid = 3:1, refluxed, 24 h
Pyrolysis: 270 ℃ under N2, 15 min, 98% H2SO4 or H-ZSM-5
Solvothermal treatment: dimethylformamide (DMF) as solvent, 160 ℃ for 6 h
Microwave irradiation: household microwave irradiation (700 W) for 2 min
Ultrasonic-assisted method: ultrasonic treatment for 8 h under Ar flow

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs), an emerging fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, have attracted great attention in recent year due to their features such as unique optical property, good biocompatibility and low cost and easy synthesis. When CDs are integrated with porous materials, it not only compensates for the shortcomings of monocomponent CDs in applications, but also produces new functionalities through synergistic interactions to extend new applications. At present, CDs have been assembled with various porous materials such as zeolites, carbonaceous porous materials, porous graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), mesoporous SiO2, porous metallic compounds and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), which exhibit excellent performance and a wide range of applications. Therefore, recent research advances in CDs/porous materials composites should be presented to facilitate further development of the field. In this article, the preparation methods, porous structures, and properties of CDs/porous materials composites are classified and presented. Then, the applications of CDs/porous materials composites in optics, biomedicine, sensing, electrochemistry, and photocatalysis are also discussed. Finally, current challenges and future directions are briefly discussed to facilitate the next development of CDs/porous materials composites.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), an emerging fluorescent carbon nanomaterial with a size smaller than 10 nm, typically consist of amorphous to nanocrystalline cores and abundant surface functional groups [1,2]. CDs were first discovered occasionally by Xu et al. [3] in the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004. Then Sun et al. [4] found that the fluorescence emissions of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles could be enhanced via surface passivation, and named these fluorescent carbon nanoparticles as “carbon dots”. Since then, more research work has been devoted to the synthesis, mechanism, and application of CDs. In particular, the meaning of CDs is no longer specific due to a variety of synthetic methods and the wide availability of raw materials, and can be categorized as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [5]. The widespread interest in CDs is inextricably associated with their unique advantages such as low cost, easy synthesis, biocompatibility, high water solubility, photostability and easy functionalization [1,6,7]. More importantly, CDs exhibit some unique properties such as effective optical absorption, tunable photoluminescence (PL), up-converted photoluminescence (UPCL), and photoinduced electron transfer [8]. Based on superior optical, biological, and electrochemical properties, CDs are favored in many fields, such as bioimaging [9–13], sensing [14–20], catalysis [21,22], energy devices [23–25], optoelectronics [26–29] and so on. However, in practical applications, monocomponent CDs cannot fully realize their potentials. A common phenomenon is that CDs often undergo the aggregation‐induced luminescence quenching effect in the solid state, which results from π–π interactions of CDs’ graphitized cores, excessive resonance energy transfer (RET), and interparticle coupled surface states [30–32]. As a result, luminescence quenching of solid-state CDs seriously affects the applications related to the optical properties, such as opto-electronic devices, printing [33–35]. For example, When CDs are used as the color converter of white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs), the aggregation‐induced luminescence quenching of CDs can cause WLEDs to exhibit lower luminous efficiency [36]. In the photocatalysis applications, monocomponent CDs exhibit poor photocatalytic activity due to their low photosensitization efficiency [37]. In addition, CDs are not easily recyclable because of their extremely small size.

Recently, it is an interesting research topic to combine CDs with porous materials. By integrating CDs with porous materials, not only can the above drawbacks of CDs be overcome, but also through their synergistic effect enhance existing performance or introduce other novel functionalities to extend the applications. Porous materials typically have high porosity (micropores (<2 nm), mesopores (2–50 nm), macropores (>50 nm) or their combination), which gives them high specific surface area and pore volume. On the one hand, CDs can be well dispersed and stabilized in the materials with nanoscale porosity, which can exhibit the full properties of CDs (PL, UPCL, photoinduced electron transfer, etc.). On the other hand, synergistic effects can be generated between CDs and porous materials, which are usually in two categories. One is the synergistic interaction between the CDs and the porous structure of the porous material. For example, the fluorescence sensor constructed by CDs and metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) mainly utilizes the fluorescence quenching properties of the target analyte to CDs and the ability of MOFs to accumulate the analyte relying on the porous structure [38]. Another is to focus on the synergistic interaction between CDs and the physical or chemical function of the porous materials, while the porosity of the porous material is only used as an aid to enhance performance. For example, when CDs-modified porous graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) composites are applied for photocatalytic degradation, CDs can act as photosensitizers and promptly separate the photo-generated electron-hole pairs to enhance the photocatalytic activity provided by g-C3N4, while the porous structure of the composite is used to expose more active sites to further improve photocatalytic degradation efficiency [39].

In this review, the current progress of CDs-based porous materials composites is presented. Before describing the strategy of combining CDs with porous materials, the common synthesis methods of CDs are briefly introduced, and then further detailed strategies for incorporating CDs into various porous materials, such as zeolites, carbonaceous porous materials, porous g-C3N4, mesoporous SiO2, porous metallic compounds and MOFs are described. Next, this review focuses on describing the porous structure of CDs/porous materials composites and the properties afforded by CDs (PL, photoinduced electron transfer and UPCL). In addition, this review also discusses the applications of CDs/porous materials composites in the fields of optics, biomedicine, sensing, electrochemistry, and photocatalysis. Finally, based on the current research progress, this review gives the current challenges and future directions in the hope of providing a little help to researchers in the field.

Since the first discovery of CDs, various synthesis methods have been developed and a wide range of raw materials have been utilized to synthesize CDs [24,40]. It is worth noting that the diversity of raw materials and synthesis methods for CDs can facilitate combinations of CDs with various host materials. Depending on the chosen carbon source, CDs can be synthesized by two routes: top-down and bottom-up routes. Top-down routes mainly use chemical or physical methods to break down the raw materials with larger carbon structures (such as carbon nanotubes, graphite rods, oxide graphene, etc.) into small-sized CDs, including arc discharge [3,41], laser ablation [4,42], electrochemical carbonization [43–46] and chemical oxidation [9,47,48]. However, the top-down approaches have some drawbacks such as the need for expensive laboratory equipment, complex experimental procedures, and harsh conditions. Compared to the top-down synthesis approach, the bottom-up approach is economical and simple and has a greater potential for large-scale preparation of CDs. For bottom-up routes, carbohydrates, organic acids, and natural products, etc. are usually used as carbon sources to prepare fluorescent CDs through a series of chemical reactions. And bottom-up methods for synthesizing CDs mainly include combustion [49–52], pyrolysis [10,26,53], hydrothermal treatment [54–59], solvothermal treatment [11,60], microwave irradiation [61,62] and ultrasonic [14,63] etc. Since the synthesis methods of CDs have been discussed in detail in some reviews [5,7], we will simply introduce them in this review. Table 1 will be given below to provide a brief summary of the CDs synthesized via various synthesis methods.

In the process of integrating CDs with porous materials, due to the simple preparation of CDs, the conditions for forming CDs can be created in the process of preparing porous materials, so that the composites of CDs and porous materials can be prepared in one step, which saves time and resources. However, the above preparation strategy has higher requirements for the selection of porous materials and synthesis conditions, and it is not suitable for the integration of CDs and most porous materials. The most widely used combination strategy is to mix the pre-prepared CDs with the precursors of porous materials, and then prepare CDs/porous materials composites through one or several steps, or synthesize the porous materials in advance, and then generate CDs within it, which is called two-step synthesis method. Therefore, in the following, the preparation methods of CDs/porous materials composites are discussed according to the division of one-step synthesis and two-step synthesis.

In the process of one-step synthesis for the preparation of CDs/porous materials composites, by selecting suitable precursors and synthesis conditions, porous materials and CDs can be formed simultaneously. And this method has been used to combine CDs with porous materials such as zeolites, MOFs, porous Ag. For example, in zeolite synthesis, organic molecules are often required to participate as templates, and under the appropriate conditions, these organic molecules can also act as carbon sources to generate CDs. Based on this, Yu group mixed inorganic precursors with organic template (triethylamine or 4,7,10-trioxo-1,13-tridecanediamine) and CDs were generated in situ and embedded in zeolite crystals to prepare CDs@zeolite composites under hydrothermal or solvothermal conditions [64]. Under solvothermal conditions, organic templates and solvents as carbon sources form CDs in parallel with the formation of zeolite crystals, which eventually results in CDs embedded in the zeolite matrix in situ. Subsequently, based on this synthetic strategy, the group synthesized a series of CDs@zeolite composites with different photoluminescent properties by doping the zeolite matrix with heteroatoms (Zn2+, Mn2+) [65], varying the organic template species [66] or co-doping two different organic templates [67]. Furthermore, Yao et al. [68] synthesized CDs@uio-66 composite by one-step solvent-free-thermal method. Specifically, ZrOCl2·8H2O, 2,5-dihydroxyterephthalic acid, and tetraethylammonium bromide were mixed and ground, and then heated at 180 ℃, so that CDs were formed in situ and embedded in UiO-66-(OH)2 (Fig. 1 ). Recently, the preparation of p-Ag/CQD composite by a one-step method has been shown to be feasible. By mixing D-glucose with Ag2O precursor, the p-Ag/CQD composite can be prepared by ultrasonic method where D-glucose acts as both a carbon source and is also used to reduce Ag2O precursor [69]. Although the one-step synthesis method is simple and straightforward, it is still difficult to simultaneously control the synthesis of CDs and porous materials, and the method cannot be applicable to most systems.

In comparison to one-step synthesis, two-step synthesis offers more general applicability and flexibility. By designing each component individually and then combining them, it is possible to prepare CDs/porous materials composites suitable for some specific tasks. Below, some CDs/porous materials composites prepared based on two-step synthesis method are introduced. Examples of porous materials include zeolites, carbonaceous porous materials, porous g-C3N4, mesoporous SiO2, porous metallic compounds and MOFs.

Zeolites are a kind of inorganic crystalline materials with special pore structure formed by the co-angular connection of TO4 tetrahedron (T represents Si, Al, P, etc.) by sharing oxygen atoms, the pores of which are orderly distributed and generally in the range of 0.3–2 nm. Due to such uniformly distributed pores and stable crystalline structures, zeolites have become an ideal host matrix for the incorporation of various functional guests. In addition to the one-step synthesis method mentioned above, CDs@zeolite composites can also be prepared by two-step synthesis. In 2010, Xiu et al. [70] found that oxidative pyrolysis of the occluded organic template (cyclohexylamine) in the Mg-substituted microporous aluminophosphate host or heat treatment of loaded organic small molecules (n-hexane, acetone , ethanol and acetic acid) resulted in the formation of CNPs in the micropores of the zeolite. Yu group then used a similar approach to synthesize CDs@zeolite composites. As shown in Fig. 2 , the magnesium aluminophosphate zeolite was prepared by hydrothermal treatment of pseudoboomite, magnesium acetate tetrahydrate, orthophosphate and N-methylpiperidine/N-methylmorpholine (organic template), then as-synthesized zeolite was calcined at 600 °C to pyrolyze the organic template confined in the zeolite for the formation of CNDs. More importantly, by simply altering the heat treatment conditions (temperature and time), zeolite matrix or the type of organic template, the photoluminescence properties of CDs@zeolite composites can be tuned [71,72].

Carbonaceous materials are abundant in raw materials and simple to prepare, while exhibiting relatively high electrical conductivity and good structural stability. Carbonaceous materials endowed with a porous structure have a larger specific surface area and can be flexibly adjusted in terms of pore size and pore distribution as needed [73]. The integration of CDs and carbonaceous porous materials is not only beneficial to the dispersion of CDs, but also to obtain excellent electrochemical performance through synergistic effects. Recently, it has been reported to combine CDs with carbonaceous materials such as porous carbon [74–77], activated carbon [78,79], carbon nanospheres [80], carbon aerogel [81] and 3D porous graphene [37,82–85]. Kumar et al. [78] prepared activated carbon/carbon nanodots (C-dots) composites using a two-step method. Specifically, C-dots were prepared by ultrasonic treatment of polyethylene glycol, and then the pre-prepared C-dots were introduced into the pores of the activated carbon by sonochemical modification (Fig. 3 a). The synthesis method is relatively simple and environmentally friendly. The abundance of natural biomass has been considered as an ideal precursor for various carbon nanomaterials, and the use of the same natural biomass as a precursor to synthesize two different carbon nanomaterials for composites is also a very advantageous constructing strategy. Liang et al. [74] successfully prepared CDs self-modified nitrogen, sulfur co-doped porous carbon composites (CDs/NSPC) using protein molecules as a single precursor. As shown in Fig. 3b, bovine serum proteins were broken down into protein fragments by the action of hot alkaline solution, and then some of the fragments formed CDs. The CDs-containing protein fragments were then freeze-dried and carbonized to finally form CD/NSPC composites [74]. In addition, carbon aerogel is an attractive host material due to its ultra-low density and highly porous network. And CQDs can be integrated into the 3D carbon skeleton through in-situ assembly without disrupting the basic framework of the carbon aerogel. In detail, CQDs prepared in advance by electrochemical methods are involved in sol–gel polymerization together with organic precursors of resorcinol (R) and formaldehyde (F), and the CQDs can be assembled in situ on the RF polymer backbone during the sol–gel polymerization. Subsequently, the wet CQD gels were carbonized at high temperature to form 3D porous CQD aerogel [81]. Three-dimensional porous graphene structures such as porous graphene skeleton [86], graphene hydrogel [82] or aerogel [37,83] have large specific surface areas, excellent electrical conductivity and unique porous structures, and the introduction of CDs into such porous graphene structures to construct composites with superior performance has received widespread attention. By dispersing graphene oxide in CDs solution, Zhao et al. [84] prepared the 3D interconnected CDs-decorated rGO nanosheets under hydrothermal conditions, in which CDs can effectively reduce the stacking of graphene sheets and contribute to the expansion of the pore diameter. Feng et al. [82] pre-prepared reduced graphene hydrogel (RGH) and CDs, and then prepared reduced graphene hydrogel/CDs (rGH/CDs) composites though hydrothermal method. Furthermore, N, P-CQDs/rGO composite aerogels were obtained by freeze drying. As depicted in Fig. 3c, the pre-prepared CDs were mixed with graphene oxide and (NH4)2HPO4 (nitrogen and phosphorus sources) to prepare N, P-CQDs/rGO composite hydrogels by one-pot hydrothermal method. Followed by further freeze-drying, N, P-CQDs/rGO composite hydrogels were transformed into N, P-CQDs/rGO composite aerogels [83].

As a non-metallic polymer semiconductor material, g-C3N4 can be used as a promising non-metallic photocatalyst due to its many advantages such as suitable band gap (2.7 eV), high stability, low cost, etc. [87]. It is well known that the specific area of the catalyst has a tremendous influence on the catalytic efficiency. The porous g-C3N4 has a higher porosity and a larger specific surface area so that it can expose more active sites, which significantly enhances the photocatalytic activity [88,89]. Recently, some reports have prepared nanocomposites by further modification of porous g-C3N4 with CDs. The strategy based on two-step synthesis method to load CDs on porous g-C3N4 will be discussed.

When the porous structure of g-C3N4 is introduced by the hard-template method, CDs/porous g-C3N4 composites can be prepared by the strategy of calcining CDs with hard templates (SiO2) and g-C3N4 precursors (cyanamide) and finally removing the silica templates [90,91]. This method makes it possible to produce a more regular shaped porous structure and to easily adjust the pore size and distribution. Alternatively, a more economical and environmentally friendly bubble template method can also be used to construct the porous structure of g-C3N4. As shown in Fig. 4 a, Wang et al. [92] synthesized porous g-C3N4 nanotubes using urea as the gas template, and then loaded the CDs prepared by hydrothermal method on the porous g-C3N4 nanotubes through ultrasound-assisted method. Also using the gas template method, but Liu et al. [93] prepared CDs in advance by hydrothermal method, and then directly mixed with urea and calcined to synthesize CQDs/porous g-C3N4 composites. In the process, the porous structure of g-C3N4 is caused by the ammonia generated during the heat treatment process. Without using any templates, Wang et al. [94] synthesized CDs with citric acid and ethylenediamine as raw materials, and then CDs were mixed with dicyandiamide to prepare CQD/oxygen-containing ultrathin porous g-C3N4 (OUPCN) composites through two-step heat treatment (thermal polymerization and oxidation etching), as shown in Fig. 4b. Recently, Xu et al. [95] found that CDs can also act as pore-forming agent. Mixing CDs prepared by ultrasonic method with S-C3N4 precursors, followed by calcination, can synthesize the porous S-C3N4/C-dot composites. Novel porous NCDs@g-C3N4 composites were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of the mixture of NCDs and g-C3N4. Similarly, NCDs play a key role in the hydrothermal process for the hole-building of g-C3N4 [39].

Because of its porous structure, mesoporous silica has a large specific surface area and pore volume, and its pore size range can also be adjusted from 2 to 50 nm according to actual application, so that it can be compatible with quantum dots of various sizes. In addition, mesoporous silica also has the advantages of high biocompatibility, good chemical stability, and easy surface functionalization. By assembling CDs with mesoporous silica, the designed composite can retain both the mesoporous structure and the unique fluorescent properties of CDs, which can be used as a multi-functional platform.

One strategy for preparing CDs/mesoporous SiO2 composites is to mix the pre-prepared mesoporous SiO2 with precursors of CDs and then generate CDs in situ in the pores to obtain the composites by hydrothermal method [96–98], pyrolysis [99], etc. Although CDs can be formed in the pore of mesoporous silica by hydrothermal method, it may also destroy the porous structure, which has been pointed out in the above reports [97,98]. However, Wang et al. [100] filled the pores of SBA-NH2 with raw materials (citric acid and ethylenediamine) to form CDs in situ in the pores without disrupting the mesopore-ordered structure by a microplasma-assisted method (Fig. 5 a). CDs can be embedded into the mesoporous SiO2 matrix by mixing CDs with the SiO2 precursors and template, and then removing the template, thus providing enough free space to load other guest molecules or further channel modification to expand application fields. In addition, the fluorescent properties of CDs embedded in the matrix are not easily affected by the external environment. For example, Guo et al. [101] mixed the blue-emitting CDs prepared by hydrothermal method with hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) template and silicic acid tetraethyl ester (TEOS), followed by acetic reflux treatment to remove the CTAB template, so as to prepare CDs@mesoporous silica composite (Fig. 5b), and proved that it has potential applications in drug delivery, cell imaging and other fields. Separate preparation of CDs and mesoporous SiO2, followed by assembly has been shown to be a simple and flexible combination strategy. CDs can be introduced into the pore channels, or grafted at the opening of the pores, depending on the practical application. According to this strategy, the introduction of CDs into the channels of SBA-15 has been reported. As shown in Fig. 5c, the multi-hydroxyl CDs prepared by pyrolysis, with DMF as a solvent, was co-heated with mesoporous SBA-15 modified by 3-glycidyloxypropyltrimethoxysilane (SBA-15/560) to form a covalent bond for the preparation of SCD nanocomposites [102]. In addition, methods such as impregnation method [103] and ultrasonic method [104] have also been proven to introduce CDs into the pore channels of mesoporous SiO2. In the design of drug delivery nanocarriers, CDs can be grafted to the pore openings of mesoporous silica via chemical bonds or electrostatic interactions to serve as gatekeeper to prevent premature drug release [105–107].

Much work is devoted to coupling CDs with porous metallic compounds, hoping to show better performance or expand other applications based on the original through synergistic effects. These metallic compounds include metal oxides, transition-metal hydroxides, and metal borides, and their porous structures can be designed into mesopores, macropores, hollow mesopores, hierarchically porous structure and other structures. The method of constructing CDs/porous metallic compound composites is introduced below.

A simple way to assemble CDs and porous metallic compounds is by direct physical mixing of the pre-prepared CDs and porous metallic compounds. As shown in Fig. 6 a, CDs can be assembled in Eu3+-doped mesoporous alumina by simple stirring. And the two materials are connected by hydrogen bonds [108]. Similarly, CDs with different luminescence colors can also be introduced into the mesoporous alumina to prepare fluorescent materials with two characteristic emission peaks [109,110]. Li et al. [111] prepared CDs/Bi:TiO2 composites with three-dimensional ordered macropores by this simple synthesis method. In this preparation process, CDs were firstly synthesized by a microwave method, and then mixed with 3D ordered macroporous Bi:TiO2 to prepare the composite. In addition, some attempts have been made to assemble CDs with porous metallic compounds by hydrothermal method [112] and reflux method [113]. In addition to the combination strategy described above, pre-prepared CDs can be mixed with porous metallic compound precursors so that CDs can be embedded in situ in porous metallic compound to form a tighter interface. In the process, CDs can also be used to induce the formation of porous structures. Han et al. [114] introduced CDs into mesoporous nickel boride in situ by heterogeneous nucleation. Using citric acid and treated nickel foams to form CDs solution with Ni2+ ions under hydrothermal conditions, the CDs@NiB composite was formed by adjusting its pH and adding NaBH4. NiB nucleates on CDs and then grows and aggregates to form CDs@NiB nanospheres. CQDs were prepared by electrochemical oxidation and then mixed with Ni(OH)2 precursors to prepare 3D porous hierarchical CQDs/Ni(OH)2 nanocomposite. The electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged CQDs and the positively charged Ni2+ contributes to the formation of this porous hierarchical structure [115]. As shown in Fig. 6b, Wu et al. [116] added CQDs during the hydrolysis of TiO2 precursors and then prepared mesoporous CQDs@TiO2 nanocomposite by hydrothermal method and calcination. The introduction of CQDs decreases the surface energy of the system, thereby facilitating the formation of this mesoporous structure.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), a novel type of crystalline porous materials, are formed by the assembly of metal ions or clusters with organic linkers through coordination bonds [117]. Due to the advantages of large specific surface area, strong encapsulation capability and flexible configuration, MOFs can be used as good carriers for various functional nanoparticles. Currently, much work is being done to introduce highly luminescent CDs into the MOFs without destroying their framework to build composites. It is not hard to imagine that the composites, which combine the unique properties of CDs and MOFs, have great potential for many applications.

In the two-step strategy of combining MOFs and CDs, pre-prepared CDs can be mixed with the precursors of the MOFs, which is then assembled around CDs to form nanocomposites. When ZIF-8 is used as the host matrix, at room temperature, ZIF-8 can crystallize and grow around the CDs, thus embedding CDs in the ZIF-8 substrate without destroying its original framework structure [38,118,119]. In addition, suitable surface-modified CDs have been shown to act as templates for ZIF-8 crystallization and to promote the growth of ZIF-8 in aqueous solutions, thus avoiding the use of organic solvents in the ZIF-8 synthesis process [120]. Using the same strategy, CDs can be encapsulated in europium (Eu)-based MOFs in order to prepare multiluminescent functional composites [121–124]. As shown in Fig. 7 a, CDs were produced by microwave treatment, and then assembled into CDs@EuW-MOF composite by mixing with EuW-MOF precursors and placing them for one week. CDs@EuW-MOF composites exhibit PL emission behavior which is different from that of CD and EuW-MOF because CDs in the CDs@EuW-MOF composite are covalently linked to the EuW-MOF [123]. However, the BET surface area of CDs@MOF composites prepared using the above strategy would be reduced because CDs may block certain pores of MOFs [119]. To solve this problem, Wang et al. [125] stirred zinc nitrate and dimethylimidazole with methanol as the solvent at room temperature to form nanocrystals, and then calcined at 200 °C to synthesize CNDs@ZIF-8 composite. CDs are formed by pyrolysis of the organic ligand (2-methylimidazole), thus embedded in the interrupted nanospaces of ZIF-8 without clogging the pores. Additionally, CDs and MOFs can be prepared in advance and then assembled to prepare the composites. As shown in Fig. 7b, the CDs/Zr-MOF nanocomposite was prepared by mixing pre-prepared blue emissive CDs with Zr-MOF and adding [3-(2-aminoethylamino)propyl]trimethoxysilane (AEATMS) as the binding agent. The size of the CDs (4 nm) is larger than the pore size of Zr-MOF (2 nm), which makes most of the CDs on the surface of Zr-MOF. And there is a strong interaction between CDs and missing linker sites on the surface of Zr-MOF [126].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and properties of CDs/porous materials composites
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the different synthesis methods, preparation conditions, and types of porous materials (such as pore structure, chemical composition, etc.), CDs/porous materials composites will have different structural morphology. Since the interaction between pore structure and CDs affects the properties and applications of the composites, pore structure is one of the most important aspects in the structure of CDs/porous materials composites. In addition, CDs themselves have some fascinating properties, such as light absorption, PL, UPCL and so on. As a result, CDs/porous materials composites also “inherit” these properties, resulting in enhanced properties or other novel functions that can be realized in different applications. Below, the pore structure and properties of CDs/porous materials composites will be described.

Zeolite is a typical representative with micropore structure. Limited by the micropore structure, CDs are usually formed in situ by one-step method or pyrolysis of organic molecules in the pores of zeolite. Fig. 8 a shows TEM image of CDs@SBT-2 composites prepared by one-step synthesis [66]. As can be seen in the TEM image, the average diameter of CDs is 2.9 nm, and the lattice spacing is 0.21 nm (HR-TEM). It can also be clearly seen that CDs are embedded in the interrupted cavities of zeolite, which can keep micropores while show good confinement to CDs to improve the luminescence properties. Moreover, MOFs can also have such micropore structure. The TEM image of CNDs@ZIF-8 synthesized by calcining ZIF-8 nanocrystals is presented in Fig. 8b [125]. The size of the CNDs in the CNDs@ZIF-8 composites is about 4 nm, and the HR-TEM image shows that the lattice spacing of CNDs is 0.21 nm, which is consistent with the (100) facet of graphite. By nitrogen adsorption measurements (Fig. 8c), CNDs@ZIF-8-3 exhibited a type-I isotherm, which indicates its microporous characteristics. Moreover, CNDs@ZIF-8-3 has a larger BET surface area of 1302 m2 g−1 than ZIF-8 (1199 m2 g−1), which indicates that the CNDs occupy the interrupted cavities of ZIF-8 not the pores. This preserved micropore allows for greater surface area and enhanced adsorption of CDs/porous materials composites, at the same time, CDs/porous materials composites can also exhibit specific selective adsorption by virtue of microporous structures of different shapes and sizes, which has important applications in sensing and catalysis.

CDs/porous materials composites with mesoporous structure can have larger pore diameter (2–50 nm), and are more flexible in terms of structural morphology, shape, and size. As a result, CDs/porous materials composites with suitable mesoporous structures can be selected for specific functional requirements to meet the application. In recent years, the more popular siliceous materials such as SBA-15 and MCM-41 are a class of materials with highly ordered mesoporous structure. NCDs/SBA-15 composites were prepared by introducing NCDs into SBA-15 by ultrasonic method [104]. From the TEM image (Fig. 9 a), NCDs/SBA-15 composites still have ordered cylindrical mesopore structure, which is helpful in improving the mass transfer capability. The nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm (Fig. 9b) shows that the pore size of NCDs/SBA-15 composite decreases. In addition, compared with SBA-15, the BET surface area of NCDs/SBA-15 composite decreases from 902 m2 g−1 to 424 m2 g−1, and the total pore volume decreases from 1.894 cm3 g−1 to 1.360 cm3 g−1, which further proves that NCDs have entered the channels of SBA-15. However, MCM/CQDs composites are prepared by the hydrothermal method to generate CDs in situ in the channels of ordered mesoporous material (MCM-41) [97]. As revealed by the TEM image (Fig. 9c), the MCM/CQDs composites exhibit some irregular pores, which indicates that the prepared MCM/CQDs composites cannot maintain the original well mesoporous structure. It is further demonstrated by nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm that the mesoporous structure of MCM/CQDs composites has been altered (Fig. 9d). And the specific surface area (143.16 m2 g−1) and the total pore volume of MCM/CQDs composites (0.55 cm3 g−1) than MCM41 (923.86 m2 g−1 and 0.98 cm3 g−1) is much smaller, which weakens enrichment ability of the MCM/CQDs composites. In addition to the variable pore structure, CDs/porous materials composites can also exhibit different microscopic morphologies, such as hollow sphere [91,127], hollow tubular [92], two-dimensional layer [39], and rod [95]. Different morphologies have important effects on the application of CDs/porous materials composites. The most notable example is that silica nanoparticles with the mesoporous hollow structure have higher drug loading than non-hollow nanoparticles and exhibit sustained drug release when used as drug release carrier [128]. In addition, Fig. 9e and f are TEM images of S-CQDs/HTCN-C composites. Fig. 9e clearly shows that hollow tubular g-C3N4 (HTCN-C) exhibits hollow porous tubular structure. From Fig. 9f, it can be observed that many S-CQDs (lattice spacing: 0.21 nm) are uniformly distributed on the surface of HTCN-C. The hollow porous tubular structure not only exposes more active sites and shortens the mass transfer distance but also promotes multiple reflections of the incident light to enhance light absorption, which makes S-CQDs/HTCN-C composite a promising photocatalyst.

Designing a material, such as graphene, into 3D structure can effectively avoid stacking or aggregation between subunits, allowing the material to exhibit a greater specific surface area. More importantly, it is also possible to introduce different levels of pores into the material simultaneously to form a hierarchically porous structure, which is of great significance in the fields of energy storage, catalysis, adsorption and so on [129]. Recently, much work has been done towards the assembly of CDs with hierarchically porous materials to produce composites with different 3D porous structures, allowing the properties of the materials to be optimized for different applications. Zhong et al. [74] prepared CDs self-modified nitrogen, sulfur co-doped porous carbon composites (CD/NSPC) directly from biomass (protein) by an alkali-assisted step-by-step synthesis strategy. Fig. 10 a shows the presence of highly crystalline CDs on CDs/NSPC with lattice spacing of 0.23 nm. As indicated by N2 adsorption/desorption test (Fig. 10b), there are a large number of micropores and a small number of mesopores (2–3 nm) in the CDs/NSPC with large specific surface area (1172.54 m2 g−1). In electrocatalytic applications, this hierarchically porous structure (micropore/mesopore) exposes more active sites and facilitates the mass transfer. In addition, the introduction of CDs not only improves performance, but also serves as an interacting agent, allowing 2D graphene sheets to overcome aggregation/stacking and assemble into 3D porous structure [82–84,130]. As shown in the FESEM images (Fig. 10c-e), N, P-CQDs/rGO composite aerogels prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal method exhibit 3D porous structure with wrinkles and high looseness [83]. The 3D porous structure of N, P-CQDs/rGO was further studied by nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm (Fig. 10f). It was found that N, P-CQDs/rGO presented hierarchically porous structure with numerous micropores (0.4–1 nm), mesopores (3–5 nm, 7–9 nm, 10–15 nm and 25–40 nm) and a certain number of macropores (50–200 nm).And the BET surface area and total pore volume of the composite are 397.4 m2 g−1 and 1.14 cm3 g−1, respectively. In energy storage applications, the different levels of pores in N, P-CQDs/rGO are uniquely useful, with the micropores facilitating charge storage, the macropores for buffering ions, and the mesopores facilitating the rapid transfer of ions, thereby improving capacitive performance [131]. Similarly, CDs can induce metallic compounds to form hierarchically porous structure through electrostatic interaction [115,116]. The FESEM image (Fig. 10g) of CQDs/Ni(OH)2 shows that Ni(OH)2 nanosheets are interconnected to form the hierarchically porous structure of CQDs/Ni(OH)2, and further revealed the presence of mesopores ranging from 2 to 10 nm by testing (Fig. 10h). Fig. 10i and j show SEM images of the prepared Bi2O3/NCDs composites [132]. Bi2O3/NCDs composites exhibit a spherical structure with internal hollow cores assembled from many porous nanosheets. Moreover, the pore sizes of Bi2O3/NCDs were distributed at about 4.5 and 55.2 nm, respectively (Fig. 10k). The 3DOM CDs/Bi:TiO2 composites were prepared by coupling CDs with the 3D macroporous (150 ± 10 nm) Bi:TiO2 (Fig. 10l) [111]. The unique three-dimensional macroporous structure not only exposes more active sites and accelerates mass transfer, but also improves light harvesting capacity.

Photoluminescence (PL) is one of the most attractive properties of the CDs. On the one hand, the PL of CDs exhibits tunability, and the PL wavelength of CDs prepared by different synthetic methods can cover from the UV to the near-infrared wavelengths, which can meet different application requirements. More interestingly, the position and intensity of the PL emission peak of the CDs can vary depending on the excitation wavelength, and it is generally believed that PL properties of CDs are controlled by the carbon core and the surface states [133]. By assembling CDs in a porous material without fluorescent properties, CDs/porous materials composite can therefore be endowed with fluorescent properties. Wang et al. [100] characterized the fluorescent properties of CDs/SBA-15 composites, as seen in Fig. 11 a and b. CDs/SBA-15 composites exhibited similar PL properties to pristine CDs, i.e., when the excitation wavelength varied from 320 nm to 440 nm, the emission wavelength was red-shifted from 418 nm to 500 nm, which is an excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior. Furthermore, due to the protective effect of the matrix, CDs/porous materials composites exhibit better photostability than monocomponent CDs. CDs embedded in the matrix with nanopores inevitably interact with the matrix to change their surface state. Therefore, CDs/porous materials composites can exhibit some novel photoluminescence behaviors. Liu et al. [64] prepared CDs@zeolite composites with unique TADF property by embedding CDs into zeolitic matrices, exhibiting quantum yield of 52.14% and lifetimes of 350 ms. The TADF property of CDs@zeolite composites and these excellent performances are inextricably linked to zeolitic matrices. On the one hand, zeolitic matrices can prevent the aggregation of CDs; on the other hand, the confinement of nanospaces in the zeolitic matrices can not only effectively inhibit the non-radiative decay process of CDs, but also isolate the triplet states from oxygen molecules, thus obtaining long-lived triplet states. Furthermore, CDs@zeolite composites possess the small ΔEST values (0.22 to 0.23 eV) that facilitates reverse intersystem crossing (RISC), thus exhibiting a unique TADF behavior. CDs@zeolite composites are also a novel type of room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) material [65,66]. The RTP property of CDs@zeolite composites is also closely related to the stabilizing effect of zeolitic matrices on the triplet states.

Since CDs are good electron acceptors or donors, the fluorescence of CDs can be quenched by other molecules (acceptor or donor), which makes it possible for CDs/porous materials composites to be used in photocatalysis and sensing fields [37,95,98,113,114]. Wang et al. [90] conducted photoluminescence spectra and transient photocurrent response measurements on mpg-C3N4/CQDs composite, g-C3N4, and mpg-C3N4, as shown in Fig. 12 a and b. The mpg-C3N4/CQDs composite exhibited a low PL intensity and the strongest transient photocurrent response of the three, and further studies demonstrate that the above results are due to the easy transfer of photogenerated electrons from mpg-C3N4 to the CDs. Another group also performed transient photocurrent response analysis on CQDs/3D graphene aerogel composites (CQD/GA-15). CQD/GA-15 showed a higher current density than CQD because the photogenerated electrons of CDs in this system are effectively transferred through the 3D network structure of graphene [39].

Up-converted photoluminescence (UPCL) is another striking optical property of CDs, making them widely used in photocatalysis, especially with some semiconductor materials with photocatalytic properties. The UPCL property of CDs is to use multi-photon absorption to convert long-wavelength light into short-wavelength light, that is, anti-stokes shift [134]. CDs and semiconductor materials can be formed into composites. Therefore, the UPCL property of CDs can be used to extend the response range of wide band gap semiconductors to the solar spectrum, thereby improving photochemical properties [111–113,135,136]. Wu et al. [91] investigated the UPCL property of CDs, as shown in Fig. 12d. It is presented that CDs can convert long-wavelength light from 600 nm to 900 nm to short-wavelength light from 440 to 550 nm. From the absorption spectra (Fig. 12c) of porous hollow g-C3N4/CDs nanospheres (HCNS/CDs), HCNS and g-C3N4, it is observed that HCNS/CDs composites exhibit enhanced absorption of long-wavelength light after the introduction of CDs, which indicates that the UPCL of CDs is helpful to enhance the solar spectral response of HCNS.

In addition to these excellent optical properties, CDs also exhibit high biocompatibility, low toxicity, and excellent water solubility, which has been demonstrated in many studies of the cytotoxicity of CDs [137–139]. More importantly, nanocomposites composed of CDs and certain porous materials also exhibit good biocompatibility and low toxicity, which are prerequisites for their applications in the biomedical field. For example, Wang et al. [107] evaluated the biocompatibility of self-targeting carbon dots (CDHA) modified mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSN-SS-CDHA) via studying the hemolytic behavior of MSN-SS-CDHA. The percentage hemolysis of MSN-SS-CDHA was only 1% at a concentration of 1000 μg/mL, which was significantly lower than that (25%) of MSN-SH (unmodified CDHA) at a concentration of 1000 μg/mL. Moreover, when A549 and NIH-3 T3 cells were incubated with MSN-SS-CDHA and MSN-SH, respectively, MSN-SS-CDHA exhibited lower toxicity (more than 76% of cell viability after 24 h at different concentrations). As a result, MSN-SS-CDHA was demonstrated to be an effective carrier for drug delivery during tumor therapy.

CDs/porous materials composites combine the properties of CDs with unique porous structures, which have led to widespread interest in many fields, such as optics, biomedicine, sensing, electrochemistry, and photocatalysis. The applications of CDs/porous materials composites in these areas are described in this section as a reflection of the advantages of the synergistic effects between CDs and porous materials. The applications of CDs/porous materials composites prepared by different synthetic methods are listed in Table 2 .

Constructing CDs and porous materials into novel host–guest composites not only avoids the fluorescence quenching of CDs due to aggregation, but also exhibits some unique photoluminescence properties, such as TADF and RTP. Recently, some work has applied CDs/porous materials composites to solid-state light-emission related applications. CDs can be used as a promising phosphor for white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) due to their excellent PL properties, low toxicity, and simple preparation, but solid-state fluorescence quenching and poor thermal stability limit their further application. To solve these problems, researchers usually disperse CDs in a suitable matrix to prepare CDs-based solid-state fluorescent composite. He et al. doped red-emitting CDs (RCD) and blue-emitting CDs (BCD) into mesoporous alumina to prepare fluorescent materials (bCDs/rCDs/MAs) with double emission peaks at 635 nm and 420 nm respectively [110]. By simply adjusting the mass ratio of rCDs to bCD, bCDs/rCDs/MAs can be combined with 395 nm LED chips to produce WLED, which emits white light with CIE coordinates of (0.336, 0.258) (Fig. 13 a). Another example is the combining CDs with Zr-MOF to prepare CD/Zr-MOF composites with 37% PLQY [126]. Coated with CD/Zr-MOF composite on InGaN-based LED chips (365 nm), WLEDs with corresponding color coordinates of (0.31, 0.34) (Fig. 3b) and high CRI of 82 were obtained.

CDs@zeolite composites prepared by CDs confined to zeolitic matrices exhibit unique TADF/RTP behavior, which can be used as a long-afterglow material for anti-counterfeiting [64,66,67]. A dual-mode security pattern for TADF based on CDs@zeolite composites is proposed [64]. In the rose pattern, the flower part is fabricated from CDs@2D-AlPO composites, while the remaining parts of the pattern (stem and leaves) are encoded by benzil dye molecules. The two parts of the pattern are blue (flower part) and green (stem and leaves) under 365 nm UV light. When the UV lamp is turned off, only the flower part is visible to the naked eye due to the ultra-long TADF of CDs@2D-AlPO.

In addition, CDs/porous materials composites have potential applications in fingerprint detection. Latent fingerprint imaging can be performed by covering CDs/ZIF-8 phosphor over glass or plastic that has been left with fingerprints, as shown in Fig. 13c [120]. More importantly, when exposed to ultraviolet, blue and green light, the fingerprints can appear in different colors, which can greatly avoid background interference, thus facilitating fingerprint detection.

The unique optical properties (especially multicolor emission), biocompatibility, and water solubility of CDs give them a great advantage in the field of biological imaging [147]. Meanwhile, some porous materials such as mesoporous silica and MOFs have large surface area and pore volume, which can be used as excellent drug carriers [107,128,145]. Thus, multifunctional nanoplatforms with drug release and real-time imaging can be designed by combining CDs with porous materials. Recently, several examples of combining CDs with mesoporous SiO2 for smart drug delivery and fluorescence imaging have been reported. CDs with abundant carboxyl groups (CDPAA) were grafted onto the openings of MSNs though amidation reactions to prevent premature DOX leakage and to enhance the biocompatibility and stability of the composite nanoparticles, as shown in Fig. 14 a [105]. When at high concentrations of GSH, the disulfide binds linking the CDPAA and MSNs were broken though redox reactions, thereby achieving smart drug release. In this system, CDs can also act as imaging agents to track the behavior of nanoparticles during tumor treatment. As shown in Fig. 14b, when A549 cells were treated with bare MSNs, MSNs-SS-CDPAA, and MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX, respectively, only MSNs-SS-CDPAA and MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX-treated A549 cells could exhibit blue, green, and red signals at different excitation wavelengths. By merging and comparing the fluorescence images at different excitation wavelengths, it was found that MSNs-SS-CDPAA could be taken up by A549 cells and that the DOX molecules were ultimately located in the nucleus to act. Based on similar principles, Wang et al. [107] synthesized CDs (CDHA) from citric acid and HA, and coupled with disulfide functionalized MSN nanoparticles to prepare nanocarriers (MSN-SS-CDHA) as shown in Fig. 14c. CDHA can specifically recognize the CD44 receptor overexpressed in tumor cells, so nanocarriers can deliver the loaded DOX to tumor cells in a targeted manner. At the same time, MSN-SS-CDHA-DOX exhibits a dual response to the abundant GSH and HAase in the tumor environment to release DOX, thereby increasing the toxicity of the drug to tumor cells and reducing the damage to normal cells. In addition, it is shown that A549 cells take up MSN-SS-CDHA-DOX via receptor-mediated endocytosis, which can increase the antitumor activity of DOX. Similarly, the excitation-dependent behavior of CDHA can endow the system with real-time bioimaging ability. Furthermore, some reports have also introduced magnetic nanoparticles such as Fe2O3 [148] and gadolinium oxide–iron oxide [106] into the composite system of CDs and mesoporous silica in order to integrate more functions to apply in cancer treatment. However, Khalil et al. [145] assembled N-CNDs with UiO-66-NH2 to prepare UiO-66-NH2@N-CNDs for drug delivery and cell fluorescence imaging. UiO-66-NH2@N-CNDs with micropores can be used to load acid (RA), and exhibit a sustained release to RA at different pH values. Due to the fluorescent properties of CNDs, this carrier can be used for cell fluorescence imaging. By comparing Fig. 14d with e, the green fluorescence emitted by UiO-66-NH2@N-CNDs indicates that it entered A549 cells and was located in the cytoplasm. More interestingly, the fluorescence emitted by N-CNDs under ultraviolet light increases the apoptosis effect on A549 cancer cell line and can reduce the use of drug.

In recent years, chemosensors designed to integrate CDs with porous materials have shown unique advantages. In particular, CDs act as excellent fluorescent probes, while porous materials can not only be used as carriers for loading CDs, but also enrich analytes by virtue of their porous structure to improve the sensitivity of the sensor. For example, one of the greatest difficulties in detecting gas is its low concentration. Wang et al. [97] solved this problem well by combining CQDs with MCM-41 into composites (MCM/CQD). MCM/CQD can combine the porosity of MCM-41 with distinctive properties of CQDs. When acetic acid gas and acetic acid solution were tested, MCM/CQD was found to be more sensitive to the detection of acetic acid gas (detection limit: 0.2 μmol/L) than acetic acid solution (detection limit: 3 μmol/L). This is mainly because the porous structure of MCM-41 can enrich more acetic acid gas molecules around the CDs, thus enhancing the fluorescent response and improving the detection sensitivity. In addition, the introduction of yellow-emitting (YCDs) and blue-emitting CDs (BCDs) in the ZIF-8 matrix can be used to construct a ratiometric fluorescent sensor (Fig. 15 b) [38]. BYCDs@ZIF-8 exhibited dual emission at 440 nm and 565 nm upon excitation of 365 nm, which belongs to BCDs and YCDs, respectively. The presence of Cu2+ quenched the fluorescence of BCDs and enhanced the fluorescence of YCDs. However, when Glutathione (GSH) was added to the Cu2+-BYCDs@ZIF-8 system, GSH interacted with Cu2+, which restored the fluorescence of both CDs to their original state. Based on this detection mechanism and the selective adsorption and enrichment of analytes by the porous structure of ZIF-8, BYCDs@ZIF-8 is highly selective for glutathione in a linear range (3–25 nM) and has a low detection limit (0.90 nM). CDs can be co-doped with Eu3+ into MOFs [122] or directly into Eu3+-based MOFs [121,144] to construct dual emission fluorescent probes. As shown in Fig. 15a, CDs@Eu-MOF nanocomposites were successfully prepared by encapsulating CDs in Eu-MOFs [121]. CDs@Eu-MOFs can exhibit the peak of 467 nm corresponding to CDs and the peaks at 590 nm and 616 nm from Eu3+ upon excitation of 390 nm. The unique quenching mechanism of Cr (VI) on CDs enables the sensor to display high sensitivity. In addition, the porous Eu-MOFs not only help to enrich Cr (VI) ions, but also stabilizes and disperses the CDs. By the synergistic effect of both, the wide linear range of Cr (VI) detected by CDs@Eu-MOFs fluorescence probes is from 2 to 100 μM with a low detection limit of 0.21 μM. In addition, porous materials can provide a platform for designing more complex sensors to improve their performance. As shown in Fig. 15c, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on molecularly imprinted technology (B/CDs@mMIP) has been reported [149]. Embedding B-CDs in silica nanoparticles (B-CDs@SiO2) prevents the B-CDs from being affected by the external environment to ensure the reliability of the reference signal. Meanwhile, the surface of B-CDs@SiO2 was covered with mesoporous imprinted polymer layer of embedded Y-CDs with penicillin-G (PNG)-specific recognition sites. Due to ratiometric sensing and mesoporous structure, when using B/YCDs@mMIP to detect PNG, it can produce a visible fluorescence signal change and has high sensitivity (the linear response range: 1–32 nM and detection limit: 0.34 nM). Moreover, B/YCDs@mMIP exhibits high resistance to analog interference and specifically identifies PNGs.

Among the various energy storage devices, supercapacitors are valued for a large energy density, fast charge/discharge property and long-term endurance [150–152]. However, CDs/porous materials composites can be used as an important component of the supercapacitor-electrode. Porous materials such as carbonaceous porous materials and porous metallic compounds have large specific surface areas, which can store more charge carriers to improve capacitance. And CDs can provide more active sites because of the abundant functional groups on the surface. For example, the composite (CQD aerogel) material prepared by introducing CQDs into carbon aerogel is an excellent electrode material for supercapacitor [81]. The introduction of CQDs provides additional active sites, while the preserved interconnected nano-channels facilitate ion diffusion and shorten the charge transport path. As tested, CQD aerogel supercapacitors exhibit high capacitance of 294.7 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1. In addition, it retains 94% of capacitance even after 1000 cycles, indicating that CQD aerogel supercapacitors have good stability. Zhao et al. [84] reported the 3D interconnected CDs-decorated rGO nanosheets (rGO/CDs) nanosheet based supercapacitors with excellent electrochemical performance (Fig. 16 ). Using rGO/CDs and rGO as electrodes, respectively, at a current density of 0.5 A g−1, the optimal rGO/CD electrode has the highest specific capacitance of 308 F g−1 and exhibits great cycling stability (over 92% retention of capacitance after 20,000 cycles). This is due to the following synergistic effects: the 3D rGO framework facilitates the penetration of electrolyte ions and their contact with the electrolyte, thus reducing the combined resistance and improving capacitance, and the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the CDs can contribute higher pseudo-capacitance.

Again, Li et al. [83] presented an N, P-CQDs/rGO composite aerogel as electrode material for supercapacitors. Compared with GO, rGO, N, P-rGO and CQDs/rGO, N, P-CQDs/rGO exhibits a higher specific capacitance of 453.7 F g−1 (at 1 A g−1). In addition, N, P-CQDs/rGO has high rate capability of 69.5% (at 50 A g−1) and superior cycling performance (93.5% retention over 10,000 cycles), as shown in Fig. 17 . Here, in addition to the contribution of the hierarchically porous structure and CDs to the excellent electrochemical performance of supercapacitors, co-doping of N and P can also provide more active sites, increase the specific surface area, and enhance the electronic conductivity, to improve the capacitance. Very recently, 3D porous hierarchical CQDs/Ni(OH)2 nanocomposite were used as a positive electrode in supercapacitors [115]. The maximum specific capacitance and high energy density for asymmetric supercapacitors (ASCs) prepared from optimal CQDs/Ni (OH)2 composite are 161.3 F·g−1 and 57.4 Wh·kg−1, respectively. In addition, ASCs showed about 87.8% specific capacity retention after 4000 cycles at 20 A g−1. The introduction of CQDs helps to improve the conductivity of CQDs/Ni(OH)2 composite and promotes the formation of mesoporous architecture structure, resulting in electrochemical properties superior to that previously reported.

Photocatalytic technology has attracted growing attention as an eco-friendly and effective way to produce energy and decompose pollutants [153]. CDs have potential applications in photocatalysis due to their ability to generate hole-electron pairs by photoexcitation. However, monocomponent CDs exhibit lower photocatalytic activity due to the inability of the photogenerated carriers to be efficiently separated. CDs are assembled with highly conductive porous materials such as carbonaceous porous materials, which can significantly improve the photocatalytic performance. For example, Wang et al. [37] developed a metal-free catalyst by dispersing CQDs in graphene aerogels. CQDs serve as photosensitizers to generate photogenerated carriers, and the unique porous structure of graphene aerogels promotes photogenerated carrier separation and exposes more active sites (Fig. 20 a). Through synergistic effects, CQDs/GA composites can exhibit enhanced photocatalytic activity. The optimal CQDs/GA-15 removes 91.3% of Cr (VI) after 40 min of UV–Vis irradiation, which is about 2.6 times (35.3%) of the pristine CQDs, as shown in Fig. 20b.

Besides, CDs can also be used to enhance the photocatalytic performance of semiconductor catalysts through their electron transfer, up-conversion and down-conversion PL properties. For instance, Wang et al. [90] coupled CQDs with mesoporous g-C3N4 to prepare efficient photocatalysts (mpg-C3N4/CQDs). Firstly, the adsorption capacity of the photocatalysts was investigated, as shown in Fig. 21 a. mpg-C3N4-related photocatalysts exhibited stronger adsorption capacity than g-C3N4 for different substrates due to their porous structure, which contributed to the improvement of the photocatalytic capacities. Moreover, the photocatalytic activity of mpg-C3N4/CQDs was tested by degradation of OFX under visible light irradiation (Fig. 21b). Within the same time, the degradation of OFX by mpg-C3N4/CQDs was 90.1%, significantly higher than that of g-C3N4 (8.3%) and mpg-C3N4 (65.6%), suggesting that CQDs plays an important role. Mechanisms for photocatalytic degradation of FQs by mpg-C3N4/CQDs were proposed, as shown in Fig. 21c. Under visible light irradiation, light with wavelengths below 500 nm can be directly absorbed by mpg-C3N4, while light with wavelengths above 500 nm can be converted by CQDs to light below 500 nm for further absorption by mpg-C3N4, resulting in more electron-hole pairs. The photogenerated electrons on mpg-C3N4 are transferred to CQDs, resulting in efficient separation of the photogenerated carriers. Finally, O2‧- can be generated through the reaction of photogenerated electrons with O2 and applied to decompose the OFX adsorbed by mpg-C3N4/CQDs. Similarly, Shen et al. [113] prepared CQD/p-InVO4 nanocomposites with enhanced photocatalytic performance for the degradation of RhB by a simple reflux method. In the degradation of RhB, the photocatalytic decomposition rate (k) of the optimal CQD/p-InVO4 nanocomposites is 0.085 h−1, which is 2- and 1.25-folds that of p-InVO4 and commercial catalysts, respectively (Fig. 21d). CQDs improve the absorption of sunlight by p-InVO4 and efficiently transfer photogenerated electrons, while the porous structure of CQD/p-InVO4 nanocomposites can adsorb more RhB, synergistically enhancing the photocatalytic degradation capacity.

Although CDs have been explored in recent years, monocomponent CDs still do not reach their full potential in practical applications due to several drawbacks. As a result, CDs based composites have been developed to address these issues, in which CDs/porous materials composites show many advantages for applications in optics, biomedicine, sensing, electrochemistry, and photocatalysis. Not only are porous materials used to disperse and stabilize CDs, but their porous structure and physical/chemical functionality combined with the unique properties of CDs can lead to enhanced performance or some novel functions. However, as the research related to CDs/porous materials composites is at an early stage, there are still some issues that need to be addressed.

When designing CDs/porous materials composites, the porous materials assembled with CDs should be selected according to the actual application, especially the chemical composition, pore structure and microscopic morphology of the porous materials. For example, in electrochemical applications, the electrical conductivity of porous materials is first considered, and the 3D interconnected porous network structure of porous materials is much more conducive to electron transport and material diffusion than an ordered pore structure, and has a lower cost of synthesis.

The preparation method of CDs/porous materials composites is an important aspect, which can affect the final properties and applications. CDs have a variety of synthetic methods and abundant raw materials, but there are few synthetic methods that can precisely control the size and structure of CDs. In addition, the process of synthesizing porous materials is tedious. Therefore, although two-step synthesis is typically applied, the synthesis of CDs/porous materials composites is also difficult to control. For example, during the synthesis process, the amount and dispersion of the loaded CDs are uncontrollable, and the structure of the CDs or the porous structure of the porous material may be destroyed. Therefore, it is necessary to develop the synthesis methods that can control the formation of both CDs and porous materials (microscopic morphology, pore size and its distribution, etc.) simultaneously and is greener and simpler, in order to develop high-performance products that can eventually be used in large-scale production.

In terms of applications, other novel applications for CDs/porous materials composites should also be explored. For instance, researchers can extend more applications of CDs/porous materials composites for adsorption and separation. Because the porosity of CDs/porous materials composites endows them excellent adsorption capacity, and the fluorescent properties of the CDs can contribute to realize the dynamic monitoring of adsorption and separation process. In addition, CDs/porous materials composites have promising applications in optoelectronic devices such as solar cells. CDs embedded in porous matrices such as zeolites not only have higher environmental stability but also exhibit long afterglow properties, which can provide the possibility to solve the insufficient power generation of solar cells under low light conditions. In summary, there is still plenty of room for exploration of CDs/porous materials composites. Imaginatively, CDs/porous materials composites will develop more novel functions and applications in a wider range of fields in the future.
